name: Crowley Quarry
short_description: Crowley Quarry offers rockhounders access to collect quartz, agate, and chalcedony in the Missouri Plateau region of north-central North Dakota. Flat terrain and abundant surface specimens make thi...
description:
Crowley Quarry is a productive rockhounding location in Mercer County, North Dakota, situated in the heart of the state's glaciated Missouri Plateau. This area is characterized by flat to gently rolling terrain at approximately 1,963 feet elevation, ideal for collectors of all experience levels seeking quartz-based minerals and agate nodules.

The geology of this region reflects North Dakota's complex glacial history combined with older Paleozoic sedimentary formations. During the Pleistocene, glacial advances deposited diverse mineral-rich sediments across the landscape. The underlying bedrock contains Cretaceous shales and sandstones that frequently yield quartz, agate, and chalcedony specimens. These minerals concentrated in the glacial deposits and weathered outcrops visible at the surface today.

Collectors at Crowley Quarry typically find translucent to opaque quartz specimens, banded and solid-color agates, and chalcedony nodules ranging from small pocket-sized pieces to larger specimens suitable for display or lapidary work. The quarry setting provides exposed material that is relatively easy to access compared to collecting from natural hillsides or remote areas.

The flat terrain and accessible collecting make this site especially suited for families and beginners. Expect moderate surface material and weathered nodules that can be collected without specialized equipment. Spring through early fall offers the most comfortable collecting conditions in this northern climate.
history: This region of north-central North Dakota was heavily glaciated during the Pleistocene epoch, with continental ice sheets depositing and reworking sediments from Canadian shield rocks and older Paleozoic strata. The quartz and agate specimens found here originated in these glacial deposits and from the underlying Cretaceous bedrock formations, making Crowley Quarry a window into both ancient sedimentary environments and recent glacial history.
tips: Search the exposed quarry walls and surrounding tailings piles where weathering has loosened specimens - look for translucent quartz and banded agate patterns. Bring a small rock hammer or chisel to carefully extract specimens from friable matrix material. The best finds are typically near the base of quarry walls where water runoff concentrates heavier mineral material; look for cream, tan, and reddish-brown colored nodules that often contain agate interiors.
terrain_notes: Flat to very gently rolling terrain at 1,963 feet elevation. The quarry setting provides exposed mineral material with minimal vegetation in working areas. Walking distance from parking to prime collecting areas is typically under 0.25 miles. Footing is generally stable on level ground, though some quarry floors may be uneven or rocky. Spring snowmelt can create muddy conditions; visit after dry periods for best access.
best_season: May - September. Late spring through early fall provides the most comfortable rockhounding conditions in North Dakota. Summer (June-July) temperatures reach 70-80 degrees Fahrenheit with long daylight hours, ideal for extended collecting trips. Avoid winter months (November-March) when snow and ice make terrain difficult to access and weather becomes severe.
written_directions: From Mercer, take State Highway 200 west approximately 8 miles. The quarry is located in the open prairie south of the highway. Look for a graded road or access turnout on the south side of Highway 200. The GPS coordinates (47.334769, -101.807665) pinpoint the quarry location. Limited signage may be present; ask locally for current access details and any seasonal restrictions.
road_conditions: Graded dirt/gravel road with minimal washboard. Passenger vehicles can typically access the site during dry conditions. After heavy rain or spring snowmelt, road conditions may be muddy; check locally before visiting.
parking_notes: Informal pullout or parking area near quarry entrance. Space for several vehicles. No developed parking lot; park on stabilized ground to avoid soft spots.
collection_rules: Personal use collection only. Respect private property boundaries if quarry is on private land. Do not dig below surface material or remove large quantities that would damage the site. Leave the area as you found it. Check with local landowner or Mercer County for current access permissions and any seasonal closures.
rules:
Personal use only
No commercial collection
No large-scale excavation or motorized equipment
Respect property boundaries
Leave site clean and undamaged
Check access status locally before visiting
nearest_services: Mercer, North Dakota, approximately 8 miles north. Limited services; stock up on water, snacks, and fuel before heading to the quarry. Nearest larger town with full services is Minot, approximately 50 miles south.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers and families. Graded road to parking area suitable for passenger vehicles in dry conditions. Short walk from parking to quarry. No wheelchair access; uneven quarry floor with loose rock. Sturdy footwear recommended due to rocky material and potential sharp edges on mineral specimens.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find at Crowley Quarry in North Dakota?
faq.answer: Crowley Quarry is known for quartz, agate, and chalcedony specimens. Collectors commonly find translucent quartz nodules, banded agates ranging in color from cream to reddish-brown, and chalcedony pieces suitable for lapidary work. Most specimens are found as weathered surface material or loose nodules in the quarry tailings.

faq.question: Is Crowley Quarry open to the public and is there a collecting fee?
faq.answer: Crowley Quarry is a public site with no collecting fee. However, access may depend on current land ownership status and local permissions. It is recommended to check with Mercer County or local property owners before visiting to confirm access and any seasonal restrictions.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Crowley Quarry?
faq.answer: May through September is ideal for rockhounding at Crowley Quarry. Spring and early summer (May-June) and early fall (September) offer comfortable temperatures and dry conditions. Avoid winter months when snow and ice make the site difficult to access and terrain hazardous.

faq.question: Is Crowley Quarry suitable for beginner rockhounders and families?
faq.answer: Yes, Crowley Quarry is highly beginner-friendly. The flat terrain, easy access, and abundant surface material make it perfect for first-time rockhounders and families with children. No specialized equipment is required; a small hammer and chisel are sufficient for collecting specimens.

faq.question: What tools and gear do I need to collect at Crowley Quarry in Mercer County?
faq.answer: Bring a rock hammer or small chisel for extracting specimens, safety glasses to protect from sharp material, sturdy shoes for uneven quarry floors, and a backpack or collecting bag. A small shovel can be useful for uncovering specimens in loose material. Remember to bring plenty of water, as the site is relatively remote with limited services nearby.

faq.question: How far is Crowley Quarry from the nearest town and what supplies are available?
faq.answer: Crowley Quarry is approximately 8 miles south of Mercer, North Dakota. Mercer offers limited services; plan to stock up on water, snacks, and fuel before heading to the quarry. For more comprehensive supplies and services, Minot is about 50 miles away. Bring adequate water and supplies as you may spend several hours collecting.

meta_description: Collect quartz, agate, and chalcedony at Crowley Quarry in Mercer County, North Dakota. Easy public access, beginner-friendly, flat terrain. Find your next treasures.
